statement: TypeScript uses structural (duck) typing, not nominal typing. Two types are compatible if their shapes match, regardless of declared names or class hierarchies. This means a class `Cat` and a class `Dog` with identical property sets are assignable to each other. Intentional use of brand types or opaque wrappers is the only way to enforce nominal identity at compile time.

Node.js runs JavaScript on a single main thread via the V8 engine and libuv event loop. I/O operations (network, filesystem) are handed off to the OS or libuv thread pool and their callbacks are queued for the event loop to process once the call stack is empty. CPU-bound synchronous work blocks the entire event loop and stalls all concurrent requests. The phases are: timers → pending callbacks → idle/prepare → poll → check (setImmediate) → close callbacks.

Node.js has two distinct module systems: CommonJS (require/module.exports, .cjs files, synchronous) and ES Modules (import/export, .mjs files or .js when package.json sets "type":"module", asynchronous static analysis). The two systems do not interoperate symmetrically: ESM can import CJS default exports, but CJS cannot require() ESM packages at all. This split is the root cause of a large class of 'ERR_REQUIRE_ESM' and 'Cannot use import statement' errors.

Using 'any' defeats TypeScript's type system silently. Variables typed as 'any' spread type unsafety to every consumer: assignments from 'any' widen the receiving variable's type, function calls on 'any' return 'any', and errors that would be caught at compile time are deferred to runtime. Common hotspots are JSON.parse() (returns 'any'), untyped third-party library imports, and catch clause bindings (pre-TS 4.0 default). Prefer 'unknown' with explicit narrowing, or 'as const' for literal JSON.

In Node.js, an unhandled promise rejection (a rejected Promise with no .catch() or try/catch in an async context) emits an 'unhandledRejection' event. Since Node 15, this terminates the process with a non-zero exit code by default. The silent failure mode in older versions caused production services to silently discard errors. Always attach a top-level error handler, use process.on('unhandledRejection', ...) as a last-resort safety net, and enable TypeScript's 'strict' mode together with eslint's no-floating-promises rule.

ESM/CJS interop produces three recurring failure modes: (1) require() of an ESM-only package throws ERR_REQUIRE_ESM — fix by dynamic import() or switch to ESM; (2) Named exports from CJS re-exported through an ESM wrapper may disappear (only 'default' is guaranteed) — fix by accessing module.exports members explicitly; (3) tsconfig 'module' and 'moduleResolution' settings that mismatch the runtime loader (e.g., 'bundler' moduleResolution used with raw Node.js) cause tsc to accept code that Node rejects. Always align tsconfig, package.json 'type', and file extensions.

TypeScript's 'strict' flag is a shorthand that enables eight sub-flags: strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, strictBindCallApply, strictPropertyInitialization, noImplicitAny, noImplicitThis, alwaysStrict, and useUnknownInCatchVariables. Each prevents a distinct class of runtime error. Teams that enable strict incrementally (per-file via @ts-strict-ignore or per-project via strictNullChecks alone) gain safety progressively without rewriting the entire codebase. 'noUncheckedIndexedAccess' and 'exactOptionalPropertyTypes' are additional high-value flags not included in 'strict'.

TypeScript narrows union types inside conditional blocks using: typeof guards (primitives), instanceof guards (class instances), in operator guards (property existence), discriminated unions (shared literal tag field), and user-defined type predicates (is Foo). The discriminated union pattern — a shared 'kind' or 'type' literal field — is the most scalable narrowing pattern for sum types because TypeScript can exhaustively check every branch and flag unhandled cases when combined with a 'never' exhaustiveness check.

Generic constraints (extends) restrict the set of types a type parameter can accept and unlock access to specific properties inside the generic function. Without a constraint, T is opaque: you cannot access .length or call .toString() on it safely. Common constraint patterns: T extends object (non-primitive), T extends keyof U (index access safety), T extends Record<string, unknown> (object literal safety), and conditional types (T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T) for unwrapping. Over-constraining narrows reusability; under-constraining loses type information.

In async/await code, errors surface only if the awaited expression is wrapped in try/catch or the returned Promise has .catch(). Three common patterns: (1) try/catch per operation for fine-grained recovery; (2) Result type (Either monad) returning {ok, error} to avoid exceptions crossing module boundaries; (3) top-level Promise chain with a single .catch() for scripts. Mixing async/await with raw .then()/.catch() chains in the same call site creates confusing error propagation. Never fire-and-forget an async function that can throw without a .catch() attached.

Adding "type":"module" to package.json converts every .js file in that package to ESM. The migration checklist: (1) rename require() calls to import statements; (2) add .js extensions to all relative imports (even when source is .ts — tsc emits .js); (3) replace __dirname/__filename with import.meta.url + fileURLToPath(); (4) replace dynamic require() with import(); (5) update tsconfig 'module' to 'NodeNext' or 'ESNext' and 'moduleResolution' to 'NodeNext'; (6) audit all dependencies for ESM compatibility. The extension rule surprises most developers because TypeScript resolves .ts files but the emitted JS must reference .js.

Node.js Readable streams produce data faster than Writable streams can consume it if backpressure is not respected. The Writable.write() method returns false when the internal buffer is full; the producer must pause until the 'drain' event fires. Piping (readable.pipe(writable)) handles backpressure automatically and is the recommended pattern for most use cases. With async iterators (for await...of readable), the event loop naturally applies backpressure because each iteration awaits the next chunk. Ignoring backpressure causes unbounded memory growth and eventual OOM crashes in high-throughput pipelines.

Node.js offers two multi-process/thread primitives with different tradeoffs. Cluster forks multiple OS processes that each run the full event loop and share a server port via the OS; it scales request handling for I/O-bound workloads and achieves process isolation (a crash in one worker does not kill others). Worker threads (worker_threads module) run JS in separate V8 isolates within the same process and can share memory via SharedArrayBuffer; they are suited for CPU-bound tasks (image processing, crypto, parsing) where fork overhead and IPC serialization cost would dominate. Use cluster for web servers, Worker threads for compute offload.

Nx and Turborepo are the two dominant TypeScript/Node.js monorepo build orchestrators. Both cache task outputs (build, test, lint) locally and in CI by fingerprinting inputs, making incremental builds near-instant. Key architectural difference: Nx uses a project graph with plugin-based inference and supports code generation; Turborepo is pipeline-focused with simpler configuration and better pnpm workspace integration. Both require careful tsconfig path alias setup (compilerOptions.paths) and package boundary enforcement. The workspace protocol ("workspace:*") in pnpm/yarn replaces local file: references and ensures consistent version resolution across packages.

Relying on ts-node or tsx directly in production is a common but dangerous shortcut. These tools transpile TypeScript on-the-fly at runtime, adding cold-start latency (200-500ms for moderate projects), preventing ahead-of-time type errors from being caught in CI, and making container images depend on dev dependencies. The correct production pattern is to compile with tsc (or esbuild/swc for speed) as part of the build step, run the emitted JavaScript, and ensure CI runs tsc --noEmit to catch type errors separately. Using ts-node in production also means you lose source map accuracy unless tsconfig's sourceMap and inlineSources are configured, making production stack traces hard to read.

Node.js native fetch (available since Node 18 without --experimental flag) eliminates the need for node-fetch or axios for basic HTTP, but it has meaningful behavioral differences from browser fetch: it follows redirects by default (including downgrading HTTPS to HTTP), does not have a built-in timeout (AbortSignal.timeout() is the pattern), and streams response bodies as web-standard ReadableStream rather than Node.js Readable. For service-to-service calls with retry logic, circuit breaking, and instrumentation, a wrapper library like undici (which Node's native fetch is built on) or got provides far richer control. The key decision: use native fetch for simple cases; use undici's Pool/Client for connection-pooled high-throughput inter-service calls.

Zod is the de facto runtime validation library for TypeScript because it derives static types from schema definitions, eliminating the dual-maintenance problem (define once, get both a validator and a TypeScript type). The critical integration points: (1) use z.infer<typeof schema> to get the TypeScript type from the schema rather than duplicating types; (2) parse() throws on validation failure while safeParse() returns a discriminated union — use safeParse in server handlers to return structured errors; (3) z.object().passthrough() allows unknown keys through while z.object().strip() (default) removes them — strip is the secure default for API input; (4) ZodError.flatten() gives a field-level error map suitable for form validation responses. Valibot is a newer lighter alternative with a tree-shakeable modular architecture.

Memory leaks in long-running Node.js services most commonly originate from four sources: (1) EventEmitter listeners added repeatedly without removal — always call removeListener() or use once() for one-time subscriptions, and set emitter.setMaxListeners() appropriately; (2) global Maps/Sets used as in-process caches that grow unboundedly — use a library with TTL eviction (lru-cache); (3) closures in timers/intervals capturing large objects — always clearInterval() on teardown; (4) stream errors without error handlers causing internal buffers to be retained. Diagnosis: use process.memoryUsage() snapshots over time, heapdump via v8.writeHeapSnapshot(), or the --inspect flag with Chrome DevTools Heap Snapshots to identify object retention trees.

TypeScript's satisfies operator (introduced in 4.9) validates that an expression matches a type without widening the inferred type. The key difference from type assertion (as Type) and explicit annotation: `const config = { port: 3000 } satisfies ServerConfig` keeps port inferred as the literal type 3000, not the wider number that an annotation would produce, while still ensuring all required keys of ServerConfig are present. This is critical for configuration objects, color palettes, or route maps where you want exhaustive key checking but also want autocomplete on the specific literal values. Use satisfies when you need the guarantees of annotation without losing literal narrowing.

Fastify outperforms Express in throughput-per-core benchmarks by 2-3x for typical JSON API workloads because it uses a radix tree router (find-my-way), schema-based JSON serialization via fast-json-stringify (which avoids JSON.stringify's generic overhead), and a plugin lifecycle that prevents shared mutable state. The operational migration path from Express: Fastify's request/reply API is similar enough for most handlers, but middleware must be converted to hooks (onRequest, preHandler, etc.) and plugins must use the encapsulation model. Express plugins that mutate req/res prototypes directly will not work in Fastify. For greenfield high-throughput APIs, Fastify's built-in validation (ajv), serialization, and TypeScript support make it the pragmatic choice.

Node.js AsyncLocalStorage (part of async_hooks, stable since Node 16) enables context propagation across async boundaries without passing context objects through every function call. The primary production use cases are: (1) request-scoped logging with request ID, user ID, or trace ID automatically attached to every log line within a request; (2) multi-tenant request isolation in shared connection pool scenarios; (3) distributed tracing context propagation (OpenTelemetry uses it internally). The pattern: create an AsyncLocalStorage instance, wrap each incoming request in als.run(context, handler), and call als.getStore() anywhere in the async call tree. Performance overhead is negligible in Node 18+ after optimizations to the async context tracking.

TypeScript path aliases configured in tsconfig.json (compilerOptions.paths) are a compile-time feature only — tsc resolves them during type checking but does NOT rewrite import paths in emitted JavaScript. This means `import { x } from '@app/utils'` compiles without error but fails at runtime with MODULE_NOT_FOUND. The fix depends on your build tool: for tsc-only builds use tsconfig-paths with a register hook or tsc-alias as a post-process step; for esbuild/Rollup/webpack, configure the same path aliases in the bundler config separately. Monorepo setups using project references sidestep this entirely by using workspace packages with proper package.json exports instead of path aliases.

Vitest has become the dominant unit testing framework for TypeScript projects because it reuses the Vite build pipeline (same config, same transforms, same module resolution), making test setup nearly zero for Vite-based projects, and because it is 5-20x faster than Jest on large TypeScript codebases due to native ESM support and worker thread parallelism without Babel transforms. Key migration differences from Jest: describe/it/expect API is identical; mocking uses vi.mock() instead of jest.mock(); fake timers are vi.useFakeTimers(); the toMatchSnapshot behavior is the same. For Node.js-only projects without Vite, Jest remains valid if the team already has significant Jest investment, but new projects should default to Vitest.

Graceful shutdown in Node.js HTTP services requires handling SIGTERM (Kubernetes sends this before SIGKILL) by: (1) stopping accepting new connections (server.close()); (2) waiting for in-flight requests to complete with a deadline (e.g., 30s); (3) closing downstream connections (database pools, message queue consumers) in dependency order. Without graceful shutdown, Kubernetes pod replacement causes 502 errors during rolling deploys because traffic is routed to pods already terminating. The @godaddy/terminus library standardizes this pattern with health check endpoints and shutdown hooks. Critical: HTTP keep-alive connections must also be drained — set the Connection: close header on responses during shutdown to signal clients to reconnect elsewhere.
